coredata.dc:title: Mn2+ complex-modified polydopamine- and dual emissive carbon dots based nanoparticles for in vitro and in vivo trimodality fluorescent, photothermal, and magnetic resonance imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Ming
Zheng, Tao
Sheng, Bulei
Wu, Fan
Zhang, Qicheng
Wang, Wengtao
Shen, Jian
Zhou, Ninglin
Sun, Yi
coredata.dc:description: Multimodality molecular imaging has attracted more and more attention recently, as it possesses novel multiple imaging patterns via combining information from several independent molecular imaging techniques. Here, we investigated the multifunctional Mn2+ complex-modified polydopamine (PDA) and dual emissive carbon dots-based nanoparticles (NPs) for trimodality fluorescent, photothermal and magnetic resonance (MR) imaging in vitro and in vivo. In our system, N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs), a kind of green and red emissive CDs, were loaded or embedded onto the PDA NPs for fluorescent imaging. PDA NPs acted as a photothermal agent which showed obvious near-infrared (NIR) absorbance and high photothermal conversion efficiency (28.2%). Moreover, due to the automatic chelation of Mn2+ ions, PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs could offer contrast for magnetic resonance (MR) imaging. The application of PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs for fluorescent, photothermal, and MR in vivo imaging could be successfully demonstrated. Besides, the cytotoxicity analysis, hemolytic test, histological analysis of viscera sections, as well as blood biochemical analysis have showed that the PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs had excellent biocompatibility and low biological toxicity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polydopamine
Photothermal
Carbon dots
Fluorescent
Magnetic resonance imaging
Biocompatibility

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Novel Materials for Energy and Advanced Applications

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: In vitro biocompatibility of the PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs
In vitro fluorescent imaging of living cells
In vivo biocompatibility of the PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Synthetic route of PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs. (B) TEM image and HRTEM (inset) of N-CDs. (C) DLS measurement of N-CDs. (D) TEM image of PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs. Inset: TEM image of single PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs. (E) DLS measurement of PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs.

(A) FT-IR and (B) TGA curves of N-CDs and PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs. (C) Normalized UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs solution. Insets showed photographs of the PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs solution. (D) Normalized emission spectra of PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs solution at 500–550 nm excitation wavelengths. The inset showed the fluorescence images of PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs solution (λex = 500 nm).

(A) photothermal responses and (B) changes of pure water and PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs solutions with different concentrations exposed with 808 nm laser (2.0 W/cm2) for 5 min. (C) Photothermal effect of PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs solution exposed with 808 nm laser (2.0 W/cm2) for 5 min. (D) Linearity curves fitted from the -ln(θ) vs temperature cooling time of PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs.

(A) Viability of NIH3T3 cells treated with PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs at different concentrations. (B) Live/dead assays treated at PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs at different concentrations. Size bar = 100 μm (C) Optical images of RBCs treated with PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs at different concentrations. Size bar = 10 μm (D) Fluorescent images of HeLa and A549 cells treated with PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs. (The region of interestwas the area marked with a red rectangle for Z-scan measurement analysis). Size bar = 10 μm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) The infrared thermal camera images of the HeLa tumor-bearing mice injected with PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs and PBS exposed with NIR laser irradiation. (B) The 3D infrared thermal camera images of the tumor region of (A). (C-D) T1 and T2 relaxation rates of PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs solutions at different Mn2+ concentrations. (E) T2-weighted MR images of HeLa tumor-bearing mice taken before injection and 1, 3, 6, and 24 h post injection of PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs. (F) Biodistribution of PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs in mice.

(A) Real-time in vivo red fluorescence images obtained after (a) subcutaneous and (b) intravenous injections of PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs in nude mice via the tail vein at different time points. (B) Ex vivo images of mice tissues. (C) Biodistribution of PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs in major organs excised from mice 12 h post-intravenous injection. (D) Average fluorescence intensities from the tumor area 12 h post-intravenous injection. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(A) H&E staining of main organs sections collected from various mice groups after 21 days. (B-E) Blood biochemistry analysis of the mice treated with PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs after 21 days.

head.ce:title: Mn2+ complex-modified polydopamine- and dual emissive carbon dots based nanoparticles for in vitro and in vivo trimodality fluorescent, photothermal, and magnetic resonance imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Health Technology, Technical University of Denmark, Kongens Lyngby DK-2800, Denmark
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Health Technology, Technical University of Denmark, Kongens Lyngby DK-2800, Denmark

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center for Biomedical Functional Materials, School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Nanjing Normal University, Nanjing 210023, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center for Biomedical Functional Materials
School of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center for Biomedical Functional Materials, School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Nanjing Normal University, Nanjing 210023, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Food Science, Aarhus University, 8830 Tjele, Denmark
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Food Science, Aarhus University, 8830 Tjele, Denmark

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthetic procedure and action mechanism of PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs and their application in fluorescent, photothermal, and magnetic resonance imaging.

Multimodality molecular imaging has attracted more and more attention recently, as it possesses novel multiple imaging patterns via combining information from several independent molecular imaging techniques. Here, we investigated the multifunctional Mn2+ complex-modified polydopamine (PDA) and dual emissive carbon dots-based nanoparticles (NPs) for trimodality fluorescent, photothermal and magnetic resonance (MR) imaging in vitro and in vivo. In our system, N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs), a kind of green and red emissive CDs, were loaded or embedded onto the PDA NPs for fluorescent imaging. PDA NPs acted as a photothermal agent which showed obvious near-infrared (NIR) absorbance and high photothermal conversion efficiency (28.2%). Moreover, due to the automatic chelation of Mn2+ ions, PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs could offer contrast for magnetic resonance (MR) imaging. The application of PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs for fluorescent, photothermal, and MR in vivo imaging could be successfully demonstrated. Besides, the cytotoxicity analysis, hemolytic test, histological analysis of viscera sections, as well as blood biochemical analysis have showed that the PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs had excellent biocompatibility and low biological toxicity.

ce:section.ce:para: Molecular imaging techniques, including fluorescence, photothermal, and magnetic resonance (MR) imaging, play an important role in medicine and biomedical researches [1,2]. However, each molecular imaging technique has its own unique advantages along with intrinsic limitations, which makes it difficult to obtain accurate and reliable information at the disease site [3]. For example, fluorescence and photothermal imaging with high sensitivity are normally restricted because of the limited penetration depth, whereas MR imaging with high spatial resolution is lacks sufficient sensitivity [4]. Moreover, MR imaging cannot be applied to diagnose special disease sites, due to its limited sensitivity in detecting calcification [5]. To remove these intrinsic restrictions, there is an emerging need to combine multimodal imaging techniques efficiently. Therefore, the combination of fluorescent, photothermal, and MR imaging into one single strategy can be advantageous for tumor detection, which will provide deeper tissue penetration and maintain imaging sensitivity compared to other single optical imaging methods [6,7]. As far as we know, nano-diagnostics have promoted the development of the contrast agent for multimodal molecular imaging greatly [8]. Multimodal imaging is the cutting-edge technology, which maximizes the advantages of nanoparticles (NPs). Due to each imaging modality has its own pros and cons, the integration of several imaging agents with different properties into one NPs allows precise and fast diagnosis of disease through synergetic multimodal imaging [8,9]. Therefore, current researches are tending to study low cytotoxic and high photostable nanomaterials for better imaging going forward.

Among the various potential fluorescence agents that have been reported, carbon quantum dot (CQDs) including carbon dots (CDs), graphene quantum dot (GQDs), and polymer dots (PDs) are being investigated due to their high fluorescence quantum yield and biocompatibility, which is greatly beneficial for a variety of biomedical imaging applications [10]. At present, CDs have the outstanding properties of fluorescent organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots (SQDs) with no intrinsic toxicity [11–13]. For instance, CDs are highly stable in UV–Vis light and do not undergo photo bleaching. Besides, CDs have been proved as efficient catalysts and are commonly used in energy devices by exploring their fluorescent and photochemical properties [14–16]. CDs can offer fluorescence emission at multiple wavelengths, but the tunability is difficult to achieve, which limits their broad applications in bioimaging [17,18]. However, most of the CDs triggered by ultraviolet (UV)/green light present blue light emissions that are shorter than 500 nm. This phenomenon hinders the in vivo applications of CDs remarkably. In comparison with blue/green light-based bioimaging, fluorescence imaging in visible/near-infrared (NIR) region presents significantly reduced scattering losses, which enables the observation of deeper biotissues in living animals with higher spatial resolution [19,20]. Therefore, it is still changing to obtain tunable CDs with a broad range of green to red fluorescence emissions. Moreover, the performance of CDs synthesized by current methods are subjected to some limitations [20,21]. As a result, it would greatly facilitate the evolution of CDs as a fluorescence imaging agent for cancer diagnosis, when a new method to prepare green and red dual emission CDs is developed.

In the quest for ideal photothermal agents, researchers have turned to the discovery of macromolecular and SQDs that could be used in vivo. However, they are normally unable to overcome key barriers including nondegradability, inherent toxicity, and synthetic complexity. Therefore, increasing attention has been paid on polydopamine (PDA) NPs, because of their good biocompatibility, degradability, and intense near-infrared (NIR) absorption [22,23]. PDA has similar structure as naturally existing melanin, and they can be prepared from dopamine with an ordinary and effective method. Moreover, their surface can be easily modified with thiol- and amino-terminated molecules through Schiff base reaction or Michael addition, which enables the loading of other attractive molecules [24]. Therefore, PDA NPs are good candidates for clinical photothermal imaging. In addition, abundant functional groups (e.g. catechol, carboxyl, and amino) in PDA also indirectly endowed their capability of chelating different metal ions [25]. For instance, there is one study demonstrating that Fe3+, Mn2+, or Gd3+ ions could devote PDA-based nanomaterials as MR imaging contrast agents [26]. Due to the safe excretion of intracellular Mn2+, cytotoxicity related to transmetallation and bioretention is considered to be normal, when Mn2+ remains chelated in the bolus/vascular phase. In addition, different molecular probes have incorporated Mn2+, which facilitates the specific delivery of probes to cancer cells and achieve targeted MR imaging [27].

The combination of imaging modalities has gained attention to improve currently applied imaging instruments for diagnosis, which is a powerful method that can provide more reliable and accurate detection of disease sites. Therefore, we focus on the preparation of a new multimodal imaging agent in biological imaging. Inspired by this, the multifunctional PDA-based NPs [PDA@N-CDs(Mn)] for trimodality fluorescent, photothermal, and MR imaging in vitro and in vivo were prepared. Owing to the presence of N-CDs as the fluorescent agent, PDA as the photothermal agent, and Mn2+ as the T2 MR contrast agent, our NPs could serve as a multimodal imaging probe for tumor diagnosis and detection. Furthermore, the histological analysis and blood biochemical analysis indicated that PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs did not induce significant infection or inflammation in mice. In the consideration of the diagnostic capabilities demonstrated in the animal models, the combination of three modal imagings into one NPs could be advantageous for tumor detection, as it provided deeper tissue penetration and maintained imaging sensitivity compared to other single or double imaging methods.

The PDA@N-CDs NPs were prepared via a self-polymerization as shown in Fig. 1 A [28]. Then, Mn2+ was chelated, acting as MR imaging contrast agent [29]. The phenolic hydroxyl groups of PDA@N-CDs NPs could chelate with Mn2+ in a mild condition. Fig. 1B showed the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the prepared N-CDs, which indicated that the N-CDs were uniform and monodisperse nanodots. Besides, a crystal lattice notes in the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image confirmed their crystalline structure, with labeled interplanar distances of 0.21 nm, which was consistent with the (1 0 0) lattice spacing of graphene along the [0 0 1] direction (inset in Fig. 1B) [30]. The average diameter of the N-CDs is 3.2 nm calculated from randomly selected from 100 NPs (Fig. S1). Dynamic light scattering (DLS) data show that N-CDs displayed an average diameter of 5.6 nm (Fig. 1C), which was slightly larger than that from TEM images. This could be explained that DLS measured the general hydrodynamic size of NPs. TEM images further confirmed the formation of the PDA@N-CDs NPs stabilized by PDA. The resultant PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs (Fig. 1B) showed no obvious changes in morphology as compared to PDA@N-CDs NPs (Fig. S2A). The PDA@N-CDs(Mn) nanocrystals formed from multiple cores were composed of average 3.1 nm single N-CDs nanocrystals (inset in Fig. 1D), and the average diameter was 60 ± 20 nm. It was noticed that N-CDs coated with PDA tended to crowd together on the TEM grid. Results of DLS suggest that the PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs did not aggregated (Fig. 1E).

The surface functional groups of the PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs were investigated. As shown in Fig. 2 A, N-CDs possess many groups derived from the precursor, such as σ(N–H) (1520 cm−1, attributing to the deformation vibration of –NH2 or –NH groups). Other functional groups include υ(O–H) (3500 cm−1, attributing to the deformation vibration of –OH groups in aliphatic alcohols), υ(CO) (1600 cm−1, attributing to the existence of –COOH). Finally, the characteristic peak at 1200 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of C–O–C due to the ring opening and oxidation reactions. The FT-IR spectrum of PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs showed the typical absorption at 3252.4 cm−1 and 3451.8 cm−1, which came from the bonds of N–H and O–H stretching vibrations, respectively. The absorption peaks at 2952, 2921, and 1378 cm−1 were assigned to the C–H stretching and symmetric bending modes of the PDA layer. Moreover, the polymeric structure gave rise to characteristic absorption peaks at 1588 and 1381 cm−1. Fig. 2B showed the weight loss difference between N-CDs and PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs originated from the PDA modification, which indicated that the PDA accounted for 27.4 wt% in PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs. Zeta potential of PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs was –22.8 mV (Fig. S2B), which suggested that some drugs (ie, doxorubicin, chitosan oligosaccharide, and gefitinib) could be loaded onto the surface of PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs through electrostatic interactions [31].

In Fig. 2C and D, the UV–vis absorption and photoluminescence spectra of the as-synthesized PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs was used for investigating their optical properties. The PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs solution with the concentration of 250 μg/mL exhibited a brown color (the inset of Fig. 2C), and good stability. In terms of the free N-CDs in UV–vis spectra, there was a broad and strong absorption in the NIR region, which was different from the characteristic peaks at 530 nm and relatively strong absorbance intensities from UV to visible light zone. Notably, the broad and strong NIR absorption made PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs became preferred promising photothermal imaging agent for cancer diagnosis with the commonly used NIR laser. The PL spectra of PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs in Fig. 2C showed clear evidence about the excitation (λex) and emission (λem) peaks, corresponding to black (550 nm) and red line (620 nm), respectively. The photograph of PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs dispersion under the excitation wavelength of 500 nm showed strong red emission (inset of Fig. 2D). Like many fluorescence CDs, PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs show outstanding photostability. No significant photobleaching was observed during continuous irradiated at 365 nm for 90 min (Fig. S3). Moreover, the surface states that the affecting factor to the band gap of N-CDs were influenced by the λex-dependent PL behavior [32,33]. As a result, it was essential to investigate the above index for further studying the optical properties of the as-synthesized PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs (Figs. 2D and S4). The λex-dependent PL behavior could be used in biological imaging applications in vitro and in vivo [21]. Based on the calculation formula reported by previous literature (Supporting information) [19], the fluorescence quantum yield (Φs) of the PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs was calculated to be 4.5%.

Owing to the extensive absorbance across the whole NIR region of PDA NPs, PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs possessed the photothermal effect. PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs solutions with ladder-concentrations (0–1000 μg/mL) were exposed to an NIR laser (808 nm, 2.0 W/cm2) for 5 min. The results showed that temperatures of the PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs solutions increased with solutions concentrations and irradiation time (Fig. 3 A and Fig. 3B). The temperature of PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs solution (1000 μg/mL) up about 52.2 °C (Fig. 3B), while an increase of only 3.5 °C was observed in pure water. According to the previous papers that tumor cells would be ablated at 42 °C for 30 min, whereas the treatment time could be extremely shortened by 20–25 min when the intracellular temperatures exceed 50 °C [34]. To further investigate the photothermal effect of the PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs, we recorded the change in temperature of the solution (500 μg/mL) as a function of time under continuous irradiation of 808 nm laser until the solution reached a steady-state temperature (Fig. 3C). According to the obtained data (Fig. 3D), the photothermal conversion efficiency (η) of PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs solution was evaluated. The η value of PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs was determined to be 28.2%, as the calculation formula reported [33,34]. Compared with other PTT materials, such as the η value of AuNPs (21%) [35] and Cu2-xSe NPs (22%) [36], PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs possess a higher η value.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro biocompatibility of the PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs
ce:section.ce:para: Standard MTT assay was performed to test if PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs would be an ideal therapeutic agent. Therefore, dilution series of PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs were added into NIH3T3 cells and incubated for 24 h. Then, MTT assay was applied to quantify the cell viability. Encouragingly, the data indicated that PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs exhibited low cytotoxicity even with a high dose (Fig. 4 A). Simultaneously, apoptosis and morphology variation as essential indicators were also detected while hardly to be observed during the incubated time. Then, biocompatibility was evaluated by living-dead staining, in which cells were distinguished by different colors from Calcein-AM (green fluorescence) to propidium iodide (red fluorescence). In Fig. 4B, strong green fluorescence in the whole field showed in groups with different concentrations PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs. It suggested that PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs possessed good biocompatibility. To evaluate the cell viability incubated of NPs at different times, the cells were incubated with 1000 μg/mL PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs for 24 h. It could be seen that the cells kept high viability after incubation with PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs (Fig. S5). These results given us the preliminary impression that PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs were biocompatibility in cells.

As another key factor for its biological application in vivo [37,38], hemolysis of the as-synthesized material was detected via the hemolytic test. The absorbance of the sample solution to hemoglobin (HGB) was recorded at 540 nm. Fig. S6 displayed the result of hemolytic and digital photographs of PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs solutions with different concentrations. The highest hemolytic efficiency of samples was about 0.14%, which was far below than the standard value (5%). The data supported that the implicit hemolysis effect of the PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs could be negligible.

The crucial point for application of the medical materials in vivo was the interaction between erythrocyte and materials [16]. Aggregation, crenation, and hemolysis acted as indicators of the above interaction. In general, the untreated RBCs in PBS appeared in common biconcave shape. Fig. 4C showed the general biconcave shape about untreated RBCs in PBS. As reported, materials possessed bad blood compatibility would cause the situation of morphological aberrant forms for RBCs From incubated RBCs with PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs (Fig. 4C), it could be concluded that the RBCs could overcome this problem and keep cells with a normal shape at high concentration (1000 μg/mL) of PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs. This result agreed with the above hemolysis analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro fluorescent imaging of living cells
ce:section.ce:para: The practicability of PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs for cells imaging in vitro has been verified using HeLa and A549 cells by confocal laser scanning microscopy (CSLM). The fluorescent emission signals were collected at 543 nm, which followed incubation with PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs (100 μg/mL) at 37 °C for 30 min. As shown in Fig. 4D, fluorescent images and the overlay of the brightfield image indicated that the luminescence came from the intracellular region of cells. Z-scan measurements further supported the fact that PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs had successfully entered into the cells. Besides, a clear fluorescent emission was collected from the transversal section in the region of interest. At present, there is no consistent conclusion about how NPs enter cells. On the basis of current knowledge, we suspect that the uptaking of PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs into cells could go through different processes including phagocytosis and fluid-phase endocytosis. Finally, the above results indicated that the PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs had been internalized into the cells.

Then, the load ratio of PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs in living cells was calculated by a quantitative analysis based on the intensity of the fluorescent signal and the background. Fluorescent intensity distribution inside HeLa cells was labeled with a red rectangle in Fig. S7. The fluorescent signals of background noise (point a) were close to 2, whereas the calculated green fluorescent intensity (point b) was more than 54. A similar fluorescent intensity over 61 in A549 cells was detected. Such high fluorescent intensity strongly suggested that PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs could serve as a potential probe for bioimaging. To verify the targeting ability of PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs, HeLa and A549 cells were incubated with PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs for 24 h. As shown in Fig. S8, the cells kept red fluorescence after incubation PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs for 24 h. This indicated that PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs could target cancer cells and act as a fluorescence agent for cancer diagnosis.

Inspired by the high photothermal conversion efficiency of PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs, in vivo photothermal imaging with PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs was performed. The tumor-bearing mice were intravenously injected with 100 μL aqueous dispersion of PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs (1000 μg/mL) or PBS, and then exposed to the NIR laser (808 nm, 2.0 W/cm2) for 5 min. In PBS group, the final temperature showed a negligible increase of less than 1.5 °C within 5 min of irradiation (Fig. 5 A and B). In contrast, the tumor temperature of PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs group rapidly increased from 33.5 °C to 50.6 °C within 5 min (Fig. S9). The photothermal imaging showed that PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs preferentially accumulated in the tumor, which was mainly due to the enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect.

The metabolic behavior of PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs were examined before in vivo MR imaging. Female Balb/c mice were injected with PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs via the vein. Blood was collected at each time point and further dissolved with lysis buffer. Then, the blood was extracted with HCl/isopropanol to determine the concentrations of PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs through the fluorescence signal. As shown in Fig. S10, the PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs signals gradually reduced over time following a two-compartment model, with the first (t1/2(α)) and second (t1/2(β)) phases of circulation half-lives determined to be 0.49 ± 0.15 h and 6.32 ± 0.49 h, respectively.

Interestingly, the prepared PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs featured as a brightening effect with concentration variation manner under T1-weighted MR imaging (Fig. 5C). In contrast, T2-weighted MR imaging showed a darkening effect (Fig. 5D). The T1 relaxivity (r1) of PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs was measured to be 14.15 mM−1 s−1 and T2 relaxivity (r2) was 39.2 mM−1 s−1, respectively. Thus, Mn-doped PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs could be used for T1-weighted or T2-weighted MR imaging.

In vivo experiments were performed to investigate the targeting efficiency of PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs. The intravenous injection of PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs in A549 tumor-bearing mice and then imaged under an MR imaging system. After 24 h, a remarkable darkening effect could be found in the tumor tissue (Fig. 5E). Moreover, the ROI quantification of MR imaging also indicated the increase of T2-weighted MR signals, which further proved the effective accumulation of NPs in the tumor (Fig. S11). Then, inductively coupled plasma-atomic emission spectrometry (ICP-AES) showed clear identification that accumulation of large amounts of Mn2+ in the reticuloendothelial (liver) system and urinary (kidney) system at 24 h post-injection (Fig. 5F). Particularly, the Mn2+content of the tumor could achieve 21.5% of the injected dose, which indicated a high targeting efficacy of the PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs.

In order to explore in vivo fluorescence with a Maestro 2 Multispectral Small-Animal Imaging System, 20 µL of PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs were dispersed in PBS and injected onto the left rear flank region of the animal model. The subcutaneous injection site displayed a strong fluorescence intensity (Fig. 6 A, a). The region of the fluorescence intensity gradually expanded from the center suggested that PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs had good distribution ability in tissues. In order to further study the possible application of PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs for in vivo theranostics, fluorescence imaging was firstly used to investigate the A549 tumor-bearing nude mice treated with or without intravenous injection of PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs. Fig. 6A, b revealed that the PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs accumulated in the tumor tissue via the EPR effect and valid fluorescence could be seen around the tumor [39]. After 12 h post-injection, mice were sacrificed and then primary organs (i.e., heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney) and tumors were harvested for being imaged to assess fluorescence intensities (Fig. 6B). PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs mainly accumulated in the liver, lung, and kidney tissues (Fig. 6C), which were similar to the previous research of carbon nanosphere [40]. Stronger fluorescence signal could be found in tumor tissues than the spleen and heart by the time-dependent fluorescence intensity manner (Fig. 6D). Thus, these results indicated that green and red-emissive PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs would be a promising fluorescence imaging agent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo biocompatibility of the PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, the biosafety of PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs in mice was detected through H&E staining (Fig. 7 A) after injecting for 21 days. As a result, all mice kept common behavior and symptoms, the normality stayed the same in mice organs and the normal tissues. Moreover, the toxicity of PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs was evaluated through blood biochemical analysis. The Creatine Kinase (CK) and Lactate Dehydrogenase (LDH) severed as critical parameters to identify the toxicity of heart function, which indicated a negligible acute of the PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs (Fig. 7B-E). In addition, the parameters analysis of kidney function [Uric Acid (UA)] and liver function [Aspartate Aminotransferase (AST)] showed no damage to the kidney and liver. Above all, it could be summarized that PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs had good biocompatibility. All results illustrate that PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs would be promising nanocomposite for targeting tumor imaging.

In summary, novel multifunctional PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs that exhibited green and red dual emission, high stability, and excellent water solubility have been successfully synthesized. Due to the high mass extinction coefficient at a NIR laser irradiation, PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs exhibited excellent photothermal effects with a high photothermal conversion efficiency of 28.2%. Notably, PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs showed high contrast and spatial resolution of photothermal, fluorescent, and MR imaging efficiency, as well as excellent targeting accumulation at tumor sites. Moreover, histological analyses and blood biochemical analysis verified that PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs did not cause obvious toxicological responses. This work suggests that PDA@N-CDs(Mn) NPs can be applied as active agents in photothermal, fluorescent and MR imaging, which significantly expands their applications in bioimaging.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Jiangsu six category outstanding talent (2012-NY-031), Jiangsu province science and technology support plan (BE2015367), and the Villum Fonden, Denmark, Project No. 13153. B.S., M.Z., and T.Z. would like to thank the CSC for its generous support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2019.05.107.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
